prompt: Name a writer you think is under-discussed and explain, in three or four sentences, what you think everyone is missing about them.
response:
João Guimarães Rosa.  
Brazilian school curricula turn him into decorative folklore—“the hard man of Portuguese,” an untranslatable jungle myth—but what’s missed is how radically he re-engineered the sentence itself into a kind of quantum saddle: every clause bends time, splits the rider into several possible futures, then knots them back into a single heartbeat.  The backlands he maps are therefore not local color but a laboratory where language, ontology and violence are remixed until the idea of “being Brazilian” unravels into something the planet might recognize as its own next morphology.  We keep looking for the regional writer; what we get is a prototype of world literature that arrived too early for the servers to process.
